citation: Van de Casteele F, Deprez D, Van Haaren J, Derave W, Lievens E. In professional football the decline in high-intensity running activities from first to second half is more pronounced in players with a fast muscle typology. Scand J Med Sci Sports. 2023;00:1-12.
abstract: This observational study examined muscle typology in 147 male professional football players competing in the Belgian Jupiler Pro League using noninvasive proton magnetic resonance spectroscopy (1H-MRS) to measure soleus and gastrocnemius carnosine content as a proxy for muscle fiber type. Players were classified as slow (ST), intermediate (IT), or fast typology (FT) and tracked across 485 official games over four seasons for first- and second-half running output across multiple speed, acceleration, and deceleration zones. No significant differences in muscle typology were found between playing positions (p=0.412). FT players covered 10.9% more high-acceleration distance (>3 m/s²) in the first half than ST players (p=0.021), and the decline in high-acceleration distance from first to second half was significantly larger for FT players (-12.4%) than ST (-7.7%; p=0.006) or IT players (-7.3%; p=0.010). FT players also tended to show larger declines in high-intensity running (>15 km/h), medium acceleration, and high deceleration zones compared to ST players, and FT players jumped higher than ST players on the countermovement jump.
tldr: Professional football players exhibit wide variation in muscle typology regardless of playing position, and players with a fast muscle typology (FT) show a meaningfully larger decline in high-intensity running and acceleration activities from the first to second half of matches compared to slow-typology (ST) players. This suggests FT players fatigue more toward the end of games. Coaches should monitor FT players for second-half performance drops and have quality substitutes ready for those positions, particularly given that deteriorating high-intensity output in FT players may also signal elevated muscle injury risk.
methods: Observational cohort study. Muscle typology estimated via 1H-MRS carnosine measurement in soleus and gastrocnemius of 147 Belgian first-division male players; players classified as ST (z-score ≤-0.5), IT (-0.5 to 0.5), or FT (≥0.5). Spatiotemporal tracking data collected via SportVU semiautomatic video system across 485 official games (2017-2021); only outfield players with ≥75 min playtime and ≥5 qualifying games were included (n=66). Distances per minute were compared between first and second half across 11 intensity zones including strolling, walking, jogging, running, high-speed, sprinting, medium/high acceleration, and medium/high deceleration. Off-field testing included 10 m sprint, VO2peak, and countermovement jump. One-way ANOVA and Kruskal-Wallis tests used for between-group comparisons.
findings: No differences in muscle typology distribution were found between positions (p=0.412). FT players covered 10.9% more high-acceleration distance (>3 m/s²) in the first half than ST players (p=0.021). The decline in high-acceleration distance from first to second half was -12.4% (FT) vs -7.7% (ST; p=0.006) and -7.3% (IT; p=0.010). FT players tended to show larger second-half declines in high-intensity running (-11.2% vs -7.1%), high deceleration (-12.7% vs -8.1%), and medium acceleration (-11.5% vs -8.1%) compared to ST players (0.05<p<0.1). FT players jumped significantly higher than ST on CMJ without arm swing (45.2 vs 40.6 cm; p<0.001) and with arm swing (52.1 vs 48.2 cm; p=0.024). No differences were found in VO2peak or 10 m sprint performance between typology groups.
limitations: Playing position and physical fitness level were not controlled for in the match running output analysis. Contextual game factors (scoreline, opponent quality, home/away) were not accounted for, though averaging across ≥5 games per player was intended to minimize their influence. Off-field testing procedures were not standardized across clubs. The relatively small sample of players with sufficient match data (n=66) limited statistical power for some subgroup analyses.
practicalImplications: Coaches should consider players' muscle typology when making tactical and substitution decisions, as FT players appear to fatigue more in the second half and may require earlier replacement. Real-time GPS or tracking data monitoring of second-half high-intensity output in FT players can help identify fatigue onset. FT players should not be steered toward specific positions based on typology, as all typologies are present at all positions in professional football. FT players showing second-half performance drops may also be at elevated hamstring strain injury risk and warrant heightened monitoring.
athleteDev: Muscle typology assessment via 1H-MRS could be integrated into player profiling to understand individual fatigability and recovery needs. FT players who show explosive first-half output but larger second-half declines may benefit from training periodization that builds fatigue resistance or from substitution strategies that protect them from end-of-game injury exposure. CMJ performance correlates moderately with muscle typology and may serve as a practical proxy indicator for a faster fiber profile in talent identification and monitoring contexts.
rtp: Monitoring high-intensity running metrics in the second half of matches can help identify when FT players are experiencing fatigue, which has been linked to elevated hamstring strain injury risk. FT players whose high-intensity output deteriorates significantly during return-to-play progressions or training camp sessions may need extended recovery periods before full clearance. The finding that FT players require longer recovery from high-intensity exercise (per prior laboratory research) supports more conservative return-to-play timelines and workload management for fast-typology athletes.

citation: Nassis GP, Girard O, Chiampas GT, Krustrup P, Racinais S. In-match strategies to mitigate the effect of heat on football (soccer) players' health and performance. Br J Sports Med. 2024;0:1-2.
abstract: This editorial addresses the physiological challenges of playing football in hot conditions and provides evidence-based in-match cooling strategies relevant to the 2026 FIFA World Cup and beyond. Historical weather data indicate Wet Bulb Globe Temperature (WBGT) values of 30-35°C are projected during afternoon match times in six 2026 World Cup host cities including Atlanta, Dallas, Houston, Kansas City, Miami, and Monterrey. Playing football in the heat elevates core temperature to 39.5-40.2°C rectal and up to 42°C in muscles, and cognitive function in complex tasks begins to decline once core temperature exceeds 38.7°C. The authors synthesize evidence on cooling strategies that can be applied during warm-up modification, half-time, and the 3-minute in-game cooling breaks to protect player health and maintain performance. Eight specific in-match strategies are recommended, ranging from adjusted warm-up structure to cold water immersion at half-time.
tldr: Playing football in hot and humid environments significantly elevates core and muscle temperatures, impairing high-speed running capacity and potentially compromising cognitive decision-making. With the 2026 FIFA World Cup scheduled for summer months across several hot US cities and Mexico, medical and performance staff need practical in-match cooling tools. Cold water immersion during half-time is the most effective method (reducing core temperature 0.25-0.35°C per minute), while ice towels, head and forearm cooling, and chilled fluid intake can help during shorter 3-minute cooling breaks. Coaches should also adapt game strategy and warm-up structure to reduce metabolic heat production in extreme conditions.
methods: Expert editorial synthesizing evidence from studies that closely replicated football exercise protocols including cooling breaks and half-time conditions. Authors reviewed historical WBGT data from 2012-2021 for 2026 World Cup host cities and compiled evidence on thermoregulatory physiology, cognitive effects of heat, and cooling intervention efficacy. Sources included randomized controlled studies on cold water immersion, ice towel application, head/hand/forearm cooling, fan and skin-wetting techniques, and chilled fluid consumption. Evidence quality was evaluated in the context of realistic football match scenarios.
findings: Rectal temperatures of 39.5-40.2°C and muscle temperatures up to 42°C are reported during football matches in the heat. Cognitive function declines in complex tasks above 38.7°C core temperature. High-speed running is compromised in heat but peak speed and passing accuracy may remain constant or improve. Cold water immersion (CWI) at half-time reduces core temperature by 0.25-0.35°C per minute and is the most effective cooling method. Ice towels covering large body surface are the second most effective (~0.1°C per minute). Combinations of pre- and mid-cooling techniques (ice towels, head/hand cooling) have been shown to improve second-half sprinting performance. Chilled water consumption alone or with neck ice towel application during 3-minute breaks helps manage hydration and thermal sensation when full core cooling is impractical.
limitations: Most available evidence is not directly applicable to football due to differences in exercise protocols, and only a limited number of studies have replicated actual football match conditions including structured cooling breaks and half-time intervals. Recommendations are primarily based on studies involving men; evidence for women and youth players is limited. Sex-based thermoregulatory differences exist, with women potentially at a disadvantage in dry heat due to lower sweat production. The challenge of additional playing time and multiple extra-time matches in tournaments played in hot environments has not been adequately studied.
practicalImplications: Medical and performance staff preparing for matches in hot conditions should prioritize half-time cold water immersion as the primary cooling intervention, with ice towels as the practical fallback. During 3-minute cooling breaks, focus should be on hydration, chilled fluid intake, and skin temperature reduction via ice towels around the neck and head or forearm cooling. Warm-up duration and intensity should be modified to limit pre-match core temperature increases. Coaches should consider adapting game tactics to reduce high-intensity activity volume in extreme heat. Scheduling decisions should involve medical personnel, and organizers should consider adding cooling breaks before extra time.
athleteDev: Athletes training and competing in hot environments should undergo formal heat acclimatization programs of 10-14 sessions maintaining core temperature above 38.5°C for 60 minutes per session to promote adequate physiological adaptations. Maintaining euhydration during matches in the heat can reduce internal temperature and improve physiological function relative to playing in a dehydrated state. Youth players competing in heat require additional protection, with cooling break thresholds recommended at WBGT ≥32°C rather than higher adult thresholds.
rtp: Players returning from injury or illness who have missed heat acclimatization training may have reduced heat tolerance and should be monitored carefully during initial exposures to hot match conditions. Core temperature monitoring and hydration status assessment during return-to-play progressions in hot weather can help ensure athletes are physiologically ready to sustain the demands of a full match. Cognitive function impairment at high core temperatures may delay complex skill execution during return-to-sport testing and should be accounted for in evaluation protocols.

citation: Kuitunen I, Immonen V, Pakarinen O, Mattila VM, Ponkilainen VT. Incidence of football injuries sustained on artificial turf compared to grass and other playing surfaces: a systematic review and meta-analysis. eClinicalMedicine. 2023;59:101956.
abstract: This systematic review and meta-analysis is the first to provide statistical pooled synthesis comparing football (soccer) injury incidence on artificial turf versus grass and other playing surfaces. A total of 1,447 studies were screened from PubMed, Scopus, SPORTDiscus, and Web of Science databases; 22 observational cohort studies conducted between 2001 and 2014 were ultimately included, covering a wide range of player levels, sexes, and age groups. A random effects model using mixed-effects Poisson regression was applied to calculate pooled incidence rate ratios (IRR). Overall injury incidence was 14% lower on artificial turf than on grass (IRR 0.86, CI 0.78-0.95), with similar findings in both men (IRR 0.82) and women (IRR 0.83). Professional players showed a lower injury incidence on artificial turf (IRR 0.79), while amateur players showed no significant difference. Non-contact injuries and muscle strains were less frequent on artificial turf, and pelvis/thigh and knee injury incidences were lower on artificial turf.
tldr: Despite widespread perception that artificial turf increases injury risk, this large systematic review and meta-analysis of 22 football-specific cohort studies found that overall injury incidence is approximately 14% lower on artificial turf than on natural grass. The benefit was consistent across both male and female players and was most pronounced in professional athletes. Muscle strains and non-contact injuries were specifically less frequent on artificial turf. These findings suggest injury risk should not be used as an argument against artificial turf when planning playing facilities, though high heterogeneity and limited control for confounders in primary studies mean evidence quality is rated low to very low.
methods: Systematic review and meta-analysis (PROSPERO: CRD42022371414) following PRISMA 2020 guidelines. Databases searched: PubMed, Scopus, SPORTDiscus, Web of Science (October 2022). Inclusion criteria: football-specific observational cohort studies reporting injuries per exposure time on artificial turf versus grass or other surfaces. Risk of bias assessed by Newcastle-Ottawa Scale. Pooled incidence rate ratios (IRR) with 95% CI calculated via mixed-effects Poisson regression with random study effects. Subgroup analyses by sex, level of play (professional/amateur), age group, injury mechanism, injury type, anatomical location, and geographical region. Sensitivity analyses for third-generation turf only and prospective studies only. Evidence quality rated by GRADE framework.
findings: Overall injury incidence was lower on artificial turf vs grass (20 studies; IRR 0.86, CI 0.78-0.95; I²=84%). Artificial turf showed higher injury incidence vs other playing surfaces (IRR 1.73, CI 1.25-2.41). Both men (IRR 0.82, CI 0.72-0.94) and women (IRR 0.83, CI 0.76-0.91) had lower injury incidence on artificial turf. Professional players had lower incidence on artificial turf (IRR 0.79, CI 0.70-0.90); no difference was found for amateur players (IRR 0.91, CI 0.77-1.09). Non-contact injuries (IRR 0.86) and muscle strains (IRR 0.79) were less frequent on artificial turf. Pelvis/thigh injuries were 28% less frequent (IRR 0.72) and knee injuries 23% less frequent (IRR 0.77) on artificial turf. No subgroup analysis showed increased injury incidence on artificial turf.
limitations: Only 5 of 22 included studies controlled for potential confounders in their analyses. Injury definitions varied substantially across studies (interruption of play vs. medical attention required vs. missed training/game time). High statistical heterogeneity (I²=84%) limits precision of pooled estimates. Studies were conducted between 2001 and 2014; one used second-generation turf and three did not specify generation. Few studies examined amateur, youth, and female populations, limiting generalizability. Subgroup analyses had wide confidence intervals due to small numbers of included studies.
practicalImplications: Injury risk should not be cited as a rationale for preventing the use of artificial turf in football facility planning or policy decisions. Third-generation artificial turf appears to be at least as safe as natural grass for professional football, and may be preferable in climates where maintaining grass quality is difficult. Medical staff and athletic trainers should update player and coach education to address cognitive bias against artificial turf, as player fear of injury on artificial turf appears to exceed actual risk. Muscle strain prevention programs remain important regardless of playing surface.
athleteDev: Facility planners for youth and community football programs should feel confident that third-generation artificial turf is a safe and practical surface alternative to grass, particularly in regions where natural grass quality is inconsistent. The lower incidence of muscle strains and non-contact injuries on artificial turf may reflect the more consistent and homogenous footing it provides. Coaches working with players transitioning between surfaces should emphasize surface-specific proprioceptive and movement preparation as a precautionary measure.
rtp: Players returning from lower extremity injuries, particularly thigh/hamstring strains and knee injuries, do not face elevated reinjury risk when returning on artificial turf compared to grass based on current evidence. Rehabilitation staff can plan return-to-play progressions on artificial turf with confidence that the surface does not independently increase risk. Monitoring of slide tackle frequency and pass selection patterns may be warranted, as some evidence suggests players slightly modify technique on artificial turf, which could affect RTP movement assessments.

citation: Mack CD, Kent RW, Coughlin MJ, Shiue KY, Weiss LJ, Jastifer JR, Wojtys EM, Anderson RB, on behalf of the NFL Musculoskeletal Committee. Incidence of lower extremity injury in the National Football League 2015 to 2018. Am J Sports Med. 2020;1-8.
abstract: This retrospective observational study using the NFL's centralized electronic health record (EHR) system analyzed all time-loss lower extremity injuries across the 2015-2018 seasons from all 32 NFL teams. An average of 2,006 time-loss lower extremity injuries were reported each season, representing a 41% single-season risk for any NFL player. Lower extremity injuries accounted for approximately 60% of all NFL injuries, with the knee (29.3%), ankle (22.4%), thigh (17.2%), and foot (9.1%) being most commonly affected. Hamstring strains were the most common individual injury, followed by lateral ankle sprains, adductor strains, high ankle sprains, and MCL tears. The total time-loss burden reached approximately 56,700 player-days lost per NFL season, with ACL tears generating the greatest burden (~12,400 player-days/season). Injury incidence did not meaningfully decrease over the four seasons studied.
tldr: Lower extremity injuries in the NFL are extremely common—approximately 41% of players sustain a time-loss lower extremity injury in any single season—and impose a staggering burden of roughly 56,700 player-days missed per year. The knee, ankle, and thigh are the most vulnerable regions. Hamstring strains are the most frequent injury by count while ACL tears create the most total time lost. Notably, injury rates were higher during the preseason than regular season (11.5 vs 9.4 per 10,000 player-plays), and running backs faced the highest game-time injury rate by position. These findings underscore the continued need for position-specific prevention strategies, better conditioning periodization, and evidence-based return-to-play protocols.
methods: Retrospective observational descriptive epidemiology study using the NFL's mandatory centralized EHR system, linked to the NFL Game Statistics and Information System (GSIS) for per-play exposure data. All 32 teams contributed data for the 2015-2018 seasons. Only time-loss injuries (resulting in removal from and inability to return to participation) were included; non-time-loss injuries and offseason injuries were excluded. Outcomes included 1-season risk, injury rate per 10,000 player-plays (games only), and days lost per injury (2015-2017 injuries tracked through 2018). Analyses stratified by season phase, team activity, body region, roster position, and specific injury type. Confidence intervals calculated using exact binomial distribution for risk and Poisson distribution for rates.
findings: Average of 2,006 time-loss lower extremity injuries per season; 1-season risk 41.0% (95% CI 40.1-41.8%). Most injuries occurred during games (58.7%) vs practice (37.3%). Regular season accounted for 56.6% of injuries; preseason 40.6%. Knee injuries most common (29.3%), followed by ankle (22.4%), thigh (17.2%), foot (9.1%). Hamstring strains most frequent specific injury (n=1,352; 9.1% seasonal risk). ACL tears imposed the greatest time-loss burden (~12,400 player-days/season; median 290 days missed). Preseason game injury rate was higher than regular season (11.5 vs 9.4 per 10,000 player-plays). Running backs had the highest game injury rate (16.0/10,000); defensive secondary and wide receivers had the highest seasonal risk (53.7% and 49.7%, respectively). Quarterbacks and punters/kickers had the lowest seasonal risk (18.2% and 16.2%).
limitations: Player exposure during practices could not be quantified at the per-play level, limiting rate calculations to game activities. Injury rates by position are based on roster position rather than actual position at time of injury, which may misclassify injuries that occurred during special teams play. Approximately 11% of lower extremity injuries had missing return dates, particularly for severe injuries like ACL tears (up to 40% missing), though imputation was used for burden metrics. Days-lost metrics reflect missed NFL participation time rather than full clinical recovery burden.
practicalImplications: The persistently high and non-declining injury burden reinforces the need for evidence-based prevention, training periodization, and return-to-play protocols. Preseason training camp carries disproportionately high injury risk, suggesting workload management and acclimation protocols are critical during this period. Position-specific programs are warranted: running backs, wide receivers, and defensive secondary players face the greatest per-play and seasonal risk and should receive targeted strength, conditioning, and movement screening. Playing surface is a modifiable risk factor—a 27% higher noncontact lower extremity injury rate on synthetic turf vs natural grass in the NFL has been previously documented.
athleteDev: High-school and collegiate programs should be aware that lower extremity injury rates are elevated during transition periods (e.g., preseason), and that workload management and adequate conditioning before return from the offseason are essential. Hamstring strain prevention should be a foundational element of any lower extremity injury prevention program given it is the most common time-loss injury. Running backs face significantly higher per-play injury rates than other positions and may benefit from specialized lower extremity loading and movement screening programs.
rtp: ACL tears and Achilles ruptures represent the highest individual time-loss burden (median >240 days) and require formal long-term return-to-play protocols. High ankle sprains carry a significantly longer recovery (median 21 days) than lateral ankle sprains (median 5 days) and should not be managed identically. Hamstring strains, while individually shorter in duration (median 11 days), are so common that they account for the second-greatest cumulative time-loss burden in the NFL (~5,800 player-days/season), reinforcing the need for robust hamstring-specific rehabilitation and clearance criteria before return to full activity.

citation: Bodendorfer BM, DeFroda SF, Shu HT, Knapik DM, Yang DS, Verma NN. Incidence of pectoralis major injuries has increased 40% over the last 22 National Football League seasons. Arthroscopy Sports Medicine and Rehabilitation. 2021;e1-e6.
abstract: This retrospective cohort study examined trends in pectoralis major (PM) injury incidence across 22 NFL seasons (1998-1999 through 2019-2020) using publicly available injury data from league reports and press releases. A total of 258 PM injuries were identified in 245 athletes. Overall incidence was 0.603 per 10,000 athlete-exposures and increased significantly over time by 0.039 per 10,000 athlete-exposures per year (R²=0.787, p<0.001), representing approximately a 40% increase from Period 1 (0.395/10,000) to Period 2 (0.893/10,000) following NFL tackling rule changes in 2009. Defensive athletes accounted for 64.7% of injuries, with linebackers most commonly affected. Complete tears were the most frequent injury type (51.2%). Surgical repair was performed in 48.8% of cases, with defensive linemen, defensive backs, linebackers, more recent year of injury, and shorter NFL experience identified as significant risk factors for operative management.
tldr: Pectoralis major (PM) injuries in the NFL have increased substantially over the past two decades, rising from approximately 0.40 to 0.89 injuries per 10,000 athlete-exposures between the first and second 11-year periods of the study—a trend that held regardless of tackling rule changes and appears to be a linear increase driven by the evolving size, speed, and contact demands of professional football. Defensive players, particularly linebackers and defensive linemen, are at greatest risk. Nearly half of PM injuries require surgical repair, with complete tears universally treated operatively. Clinical and performance staff should be aware of the high prevalence of in-game PM tears in defensive athletes and prepare appropriate surgical decision-making frameworks and return-to-sport protocols.
methods: Retrospective cohort study (Level of Evidence 3). Publicly available data from Pro-Football-Reference.com and ESPN NFL injury databases were queried to identify PM injuries reported as 'pectoral,' 'chest,' or 'shoulder' in weekly NFL injury reports from the 1998-1999 through 2019-2020 seasons. Press releases were used to verify inclusion. Two independent authors collected data, with disagreements resolved by consensus. Athlete characteristics (age, BMI, position, experience) and injury characteristics (setting, activity, grade, management) were recorded. Injury incidence calculated per 10,000 athlete-exposures assuming 53 players/team, 105 practices/season, 16 games/season, 32 teams. Linear regression assessed incidence trends. Multivariate logistic regression identified risk factors for operative management controlling for age, BMI, experience, position, year, and injury setting.
findings: 258 PM injuries in 245 athletes over 22 seasons; mean age 27.1 years, mean BMI 32.6. Incidence increased linearly by 0.039/10,000 athlete-exposures per year (R²=0.787, p<0.001). Incidence rose from 0.395 (Period 1: 1998-2008) to 0.893 (Period 2: 2009-2019) per 10,000 athlete-exposures (p<0.001). Defensive athletes accounted for 64.7% of injuries; linebackers (43.7% of defensive injuries) most commonly affected. Complete tears (Grade III) comprised 51.2% of injuries. Most injuries occurred in-game (76%), primarily during regular season (69.3%). Highest incidence occurred at the NFL Combine (122/10,000 athlete-exposures). Operative repair in 48.8%; 100% of Grade III tears were treated operatively. Risk factors for surgery: defensive lineman (OR 3.78), defensive back (OR 12.20), linebacker (OR 8.98), more recent injury year (OR 1.11/year), shorter NFL experience (OR 0.77 for older athletes).
limitations: Use of public databases may undercount injuries classified as 'undisclosed' or 'chest' injuries that cannot be confirmed as PM tears. The study cannot identify causal factors for the observed linear increase in PM injury incidence. Return-to-play and performance outcomes were not examined. Public injury reports may miss less severe PM injuries not publicly disclosed by teams, potentially underestimating true incidence compared to the NFL Injury Surveillance System (ISS) gold standard.
practicalImplications: The rising incidence of PM injuries in the NFL warrants increased attention to PM health screening, particularly among defensive players. Athletes with shorter NFL experience and those in defensive positions (linemen, linebackers, defensive backs) should receive specific education on PM injury risk, recognition, and management. Given that 100% of complete tears and 48.8% of all PM injuries undergo operative repair, medical and athletic training staff should have clear surgical referral pathways and post-operative return-to-sport protocols established. Acute repair yields better outcomes than chronic repair, emphasizing the importance of timely diagnosis.
athleteDev: The NFL Combine bench press requirement (225 lbs for maximum repetitions) is associated with a very high incidence of PM injury (122/10,000 athlete-exposures at the Combine). Strength and conditioning programs should incorporate PM-specific eccentric loading, injury screening, and appropriate periodization of heavy pressing movements, particularly for defensive linemen, linebackers, and defensive backs who are at highest risk. Young defensive players entering professional football should receive education about proper bench press technique and PM tear recognition given their elevated risk at the start of their careers.
rtp: All Grade III (complete) PM tears were managed operatively in this study, and surgical repair is associated with superior functional outcomes and strength recovery compared to nonoperative treatment. Acute repair is preferred over delayed repair for better functional outcomes and cosmesis satisfaction. Return-to-sport timelines for PM repairs in NFL athletes should account for the high physical demands of blocking and tackling, which place the arm in the vulnerable abducted, externally rotated position. Position-specific performance testing of PM strength and shoulder range of motion should be components of clearance criteria before return to full contact practice.
